title: Pulpo a la Gallega
subtitle: Galician octopus with smoked paprika and potatoes
excerpt: Pulpo a la gallega is a Galician dish of boiled octopus sliced thin and served over potatoes, dressed with olive oil and smoked paprika. The octopus is cooked until tender, sliced at the table, and needs nothing more than olive oil and paprika to be very good.
sourceNotes: Pulpo a la gallega, also called pulpo á feira (fair octopus), originates in Galicia in northwest Spain. It is the signature dish of Galician cooking, traditionally served at fairs and festivals on a wooden plate. The octopus is boiled until tender, sliced with scissors, and dressed simply.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The octopus can be boiled ahead and refrigerated. Reheat briefly in the cooking water before slicing.

content.text: Pulpo a la gallega is a Galician dish of boiled octopus sliced thin and served over waxy potatoes, dressed with olive oil and smoked paprika. The octopus is cooked until very tender, the potatoes go in the same water, and everything is dressed simply at the table. The cooking method is patient but not difficult.
Freezing the octopus overnight before cooking helps tenderise it by breaking down the muscle fibres.

Bring a large pot of water to a rolling boil. Do not salt it. Hold the thawed octopus by the head and dip the tentacles into the water three times (the Galician method of scaring the octopus) before submerging it completely. Add the bay leaves.

Cook at a gentle boil for 45 to 60 minutes. Test by inserting a sharp knife into the thickest part of a tentacle; it should meet no resistance. The skin should have turned purple and the texture should be very tender. Do not overcook.
In the last 20 minutes of cooking, add the whole potatoes to the same water. They are done when a knife passes through without resistance.

Lift the octopus out of the water and rest for 15 minutes. Slice the tentacles into rounds about 1 cm thick using scissors or a sharp knife. Peel and slice the potatoes. Arrange the potato slices on a wooden board or plate. Place the octopus slices on top.
Drizzle {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} of olive oil over everything. Scatter with {{paprika-smoked}} of smoked paprika and {{paprika-sweet}} of sweet paprika. Add sea salt flakes. Serve right away.

items.symptom: The octopus is still tough after 60 minutes
items.cause: Very large octopus, or not frozen before cooking
items.fix: Continue cooking for another 15 minutes. Freezing and thawing before cooking breaks down muscle fibres significantly. A 1.5 kg octopus can take up to 90 minutes if not pre-frozen.

items.symptom: The octopus skin has come off
items.fix: The skin pulling away doesn't affect the flavour. Keep the water at a gentle boil rather than a rolling boil for the remainder of the cooking time.

content.text: Pulpo a la gallega is the defining dish of Galicia in northwest Spain. It is sold from purpose-built stalls at festivals across the region, where the octopus is cooked in giant copper pots and served on wooden boards dusted with bright paprika. Outside Galicia it is one of the most recognisable Spanish dishes and appears on tapas menus across the country.
